/
*

July 18, 1978
R-78-07-012

Mr. Kirk A. Pessner
Dobbs and Nielson
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, California 94111

Dear Mr. Pessner:

Thank you for your letter of May 4, 1978, asking for confirmation
of telephone advice I gave you on April 28.

You are correct that I advised you that guarantors/indemnitore
of loans to candidates or committees do not become major donors
solely because of their agreements to guarantee or indemnify.

By Government Code Section 84210(g) the recipient of the loan
must report the identifying information for anyone
liable "directly, indirectly or contingently" on the loan.

If the condition upon which performance of a guarantee or indem-
nification agreement depends occurs, then the guarantor/indemni-
tor would have a reporting obligation if his total contributions
in the calendar year amounted to $5000 or more.

Sincerely,

John Giansello
Political Reform Analyst

JG:bw

log / TA /
file / ent / [illegible]
3131

DOBBS & NIELSEN
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

SUITE 2500, THE ALCOA BUILDING
ONE MARITIME PLAZA
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94111

TELEPHONE (415) 362-1940

May 4, 1978


Mr. John Giancello
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California  95814

RE:  Guarantors/Indemnitors As Major Donors

Dear Mr. Giancello:

     On April 28, 1978 you advised me by telephone that
guarantors/indemnitors of loans to political committees, in
which the guarantee/indemnification is over $5,000.00, would
not be required to file Major Donor Statements pursuant to
the Political Reform Act.

     I understand that the loan and the names and addresses
of all guarantors/indemnitors would appear on the campaign
committee's filing, but that the guarantor/indemnitor would
not have to file Major Donor Statements until such time as
the committee defaulted on the loan and the guarantor/indemnitor
actually made a cash or cash equivalent contribution of $5,000.00
or more to the campaign.

     Please advise me in writing if the foregoing information
is correctly stated.

                                        Kind regards,


                                        KIRK ALAN PESSNER
                                        Paralegal

KAP:ram


SACRAMENTO OFFICE · SUITE 544, 1127 ELEVENTH STREET, SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 · (916) 446-6752

sections.question: On April 28, 1978 you advised me by telephone that guarantors/indemnitors of loans to political committees, in which the guarantee/indemnification is over $5,000.00, would not be required to file Major Donor Statements pursuant to the Political Reform Act. I understand that the loan and the names and addresses of all guarantors/indemnitors would appear on the campaign committee's filing, but that the guarantor/indemnitor would not have to file Major Donor Statements until such time as the Committee defaulted on the loan and the guarantor/indemnitor actually made a cash or cash equivalent contribution of $5,000.00 or more to the campaign. Please advise me in writing if the foregoing information is correctly stated.
sections.conclusion: You are correct that I advised you that guarantors/indemnitors of loans to candidate or committees do not become major donors solely because of their agreements to guarantee or indemnify. By Government Code Section 84210 (g) the recipient of the loan must report the required identifying information for anyone liable • directly, indirectly or contingently · on the loan. If the condition upon which performance of a guarantee or indemnification agreement depends occurs, then the guarantor/indemni-tor would have a reporting obligation if his total contributions in the calendar year amounted to $5000 or more.
sections.question_synthetic: Do guarantors or indemnitors of loans to political committees become major donors solely by virtue of guaranteeing or indemnifying loans over $5,000, and are they required to file Major Donor Statements under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Guarantors and indemnitors do not become major donors solely by guaranteeing or indemnifying loans. However, the loan recipient must report identifying information for anyone contingently liable on the loan. If the guarantee/indemnity is triggered and the guarantor's total contributions reach $5,000 or more in a calendar year, they then have a reporting obligation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document consists of both the incoming request letter (May 4, 1978) and the FPPC response letter (July 18, 1978). Extracted question and conclusion from both. The FPPC response explicitly confirms the questioner's understanding of prior telephone advice.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: On April 28, 1978 you advised me by telephone that guarantors/indemnitors of loans to political committees, in which the guarantee/indemnification is over $5,000.00, would not be required to file Major Donor Statements pursuant to the Political Reform Act. I understand that the loan and the names and addresses of all guarantors/indemnitors would appear on the campaign committee's filing, but that the guarantor/indemnitor would not have to file Major Donor Statements until such time as the Committee defaulted on the loan and the guarantor/indemnitor actually made a cash or cash equivalent contribution of $5,000.00 or more to the campaign. Please advise me in writing if the foregoing information is correctly stated.

CONCLUSION: You are correct that I advised you that guarantors/indemnitors of loans to candidate or committees do not become major donors solely because of their agreements to guarantee or indemnify. By Government Code Section 84210 (g) the recipient of the loan must report the required identifying information for anyone liable • directly, indirectly or contingently · on the loan. If the condition upon which performance of a guarantee or indemnification agreement depends occurs, then the guarantor/indemni-tor would have a reporting obligation if his total contributions in the calendar year amounted to $5000 or more.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms prior telephone advice that guarantors/indemnitors of political loans do not automatically become major donors. They only have reporting obligations if the guarantee is triggered and their total contributions reach $5,000 or more in a calendar year.
